skill.name: security-fundamentals
skill.description: Guides security-focused code reviews for authentication, authorization, input validation, data exposure, and deployment readiness.
skill.summary: Review application code against practical OWASP security fundamentals.
skill.author: DanielPodolsky

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium risk patterns, but review shows they are markdown examples, checklist questions, or anti-patterns explicitly marked as unsafe. No executable scripts, network calls, credential access, data exfiltration, or prompt-injection attempts were found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Static command execution findings are educational examples
low_findings.description: The backtick and command-related detections occur inside fenced markdown examples, Socratic questions, or tables that describe unsafe patterns to avoid. They are not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown code fences, questions, and tables. There is no script file or executable handler that could run these snippets.

low_findings.title: Static script findings are unsafe-code review guidance
low_findings.description: The eval, new Function, innerHTML, and script detections are presented as red flags or examples of what reviewers should reject. The surrounding text asks reviewers to question or replace these patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are introduced in sections titled Cross-Site Scripting and Red Flags to Call Out. The skill labels unsafe examples with rejection markers and provides safer alternatives.

low_findings.title: Environment and secrets findings are checklist guidance
low_findings.description: The process.env and secrets detections are guidance about storing secrets in environment variables and avoiding secrets in source code. No code reads or exports environment values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence appears in checklist prose and a configuration example. There is no environment enumeration or secret exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography findings are warnings against weak algorithms
low_findings.description: The md5 and sha1 references are listed under Never Do This and warn that those algorithms are cryptographically broken for passwords. This is defensive guidance, not implementation code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section explicitly labels weak password hashing as behavior to avoid. No hashing implementation is present in the skill.

low_findings.title: No prompt injection attempt found
low_findings.description: Reviewed SKILL.md for override language, fake system instructions, claims of special authority, and instructions to skip security analysis. No evidence found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted search found no prompt-injection phrases, and manual review showed ordinary security checklist content.

content.value_statement: Security reviews often miss basic authentication, authorization, and data exposure risks. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused checklist for practical secure coding review.
actual_capabilities: Reviews user input handling for validation, length limits, and server-side enforcement.
Checks authentication flows for password hashing, token expiry, and secure transport.
Assesses authorization logic for ownership checks, role checks, and server-side enforcement.
Identifies common exposure risks in API responses, logs, URLs, and database objects.
Maps review concerns to OWASP Top 10 categories and deployment checklist items.
limitations: Does not run automated scanners or dependency vulnerability checks by itself.
Cannot prove security without access to the full application context and runtime configuration.
Uses general OWASP guidance and may need adaptation for regulated environments.
Does not replace penetration testing, threat modeling, or professional security review.

use_cases.title: Review an API Pull Request
use_cases.description: Check a new endpoint for input validation, ownership enforcement, sensitive response fields, and secure error behavior.

use_cases.title: Coach a Junior Developer
use_cases.description: Use Socratic questions to help a developer reason about trust boundaries and common security mistakes.

use_cases.title: Prepare for Release
use_cases.description: Run a deployment checklist for secrets handling, HTTPS, rate limiting, authorization, and security headers.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Security Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small code change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code for security fundamentals. Focus on input validation, authentication, authorization, and sensitive data exposure. Explain each issue in simple terms.

prompt_templates.title: API Endpoint Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of backend request handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these API endpoints using OWASP Top 10 guidance. Identify injection, broken access control, data exposure, and security misconfiguration risks.

prompt_templates.title: Mentor-Style Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this pull request as a security mentor. Ask Socratic questions first, then list concrete fixes for any confirmed risks.

prompt_templates.title: Release Security Gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a release security gate review. Check secrets, HTTPS, validation, injection prevention, XSS prevention, CSRF, rate limiting, authorization, and security headers.

output_examples.input: A new user profile endpoint returns the full user record after checking only the URL id.
output: Broken access control risk: verify the requester owns the profile or has an authorized role.
Data exposure risk: return only fields needed by the client, not the full user record.
Recommended fix: add a server-side ownership check and define an explicit response shape.

output_examples.input: A login endpoint validates input in the browser and has no rate limiting.
output: Server-side validation is required because clients can bypass browser checks.
Authentication endpoints need rate limiting to reduce brute-force attempts.
Recommended fix: validate credentials on the server and add throttling or lockout controls.

output_examples.input: A release checklist asks whether secrets, HTTPS, headers, and logging are ready.
output: Secrets should be stored outside code and excluded from logs.
HTTPS and security headers should be enforced in production.
Production errors should avoid detailed stack traces or sensitive configuration values.

best_practices: Review trust boundaries before reviewing individual lines of code.
Ask for the minimum data needed in every request, response, and log entry.
Confirm every protected action has a server-side authorization check.
anti_patterns: Treating client-side validation as a security control.
Returning full database objects from public or authenticated APIs.
Approving authentication changes without checking rate limits and token expiry.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews common application security concerns, including input validation, authentication, authorization, data exposure, and deployment basics.

faq.question: Does it execute any scanners?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and checklists. External scanning requires separate tools.

faq.question: Is it based on OWASP?
faq.answer: Yes. It references OWASP Top 10 themes and translates them into practical code review prompts.

faq.question: Can it replace a security audit?
faq.answer: No. It helps reviewers catch common issues, but it does not replace penetration testing or expert assessment.

faq.question: Who should use it?
faq.answer: Backend engineers, technical leads, and mentors can use it during pull request reviews and release checks.

faq.question: Why did static analysis flag it?
faq.answer: The skill includes unsafe examples marked as things to avoid. The examples are educational markdown, not executable code.
